observations.recommendation:
We recommended that Management require the:

a. GSO, in coordination with the Accounting Office or Inventory Committee,
if any, to conduct a complete and actual physical inventory of all LGU assets
to validate their existence, condition and proper classification in compliance
with the abovementioned rules and regulations. Likewise, the inventory
process should be properly documented and reconciled with RPCPPE and
accounting records to address the aforementioned discrepancies;

b. Accounting Office to adjust the items erroneously recorded as PPE to the
appropriate accounts. Additionally, the GSO and Accounting Office must
establish stricter review and approval process to verify asset classification
before including the items in their respective reports to prevent future
misclassification of expenditures as capital asset;

c. Accounting Office to properly maintain the required PPELC instead of
relying solely on SL in compliance with Section 13 of Manual on the NGAS
for LGUs. Likewise, the GSO must ensure that PC are updated for all assets,
not just on newly acquired items. A reconciliation process must be
institutionalized between the GSO and Accounting Office to align property
records with financial reports; and

d. Both GSO and Municipal Accountant to make a final reconciliation of their
respective records and avail the one-time cleansing of PPE account
balances, strictly in accordance with COA Circular No. 2020-006,
particularly on the disposition and derecognition of non-existing/missing
PPE and adjust the books accordingly, in order to come up with reliable
PPE balances that are verifiable as to existence, condition and
accountability.

We reiterated and recommended that Management:

a. Demand immediate settlement/liquidation of the outstanding CAs,
especially from all AOs and employees still connected with the LGU,
pursuant to COA Circular No. 97-002;

b. Require the Municipal Accountant to strictly monitor the grant, utilization
and liquidation of CAs and stop granting additional CAs unless the previous
CA is first settled or a proper accounting thereof is made pursuant to COA
Circular No. 97-002;

c. Institute the appropriate legal actions/sanctions against those erring
AOs/employees for continued non-compliance with the existing laws, rules
and regulations on the granting, utilization and liquidation of cash
advances, if warranted; and

d. Request to the Commission Audit for the write-off and/or adjustment of
dormant or non-moving account as prescribed in COA Circular Nos. 2016-
005. The request must be duly supported by the documents required in the
said Circular.

We recommended that Management:

a. Ensure that all inventory items are initially recorded in their appropriate
inventory accounts upon purchase. Expenses must only be recognized when
these items are issued or consumed;

b. Implement the use of semi-expendable property accounts for items below
the capitalization threshold that have an expected useful life of more than
one year. Likewise, make the necessary adjustments in the books to
recognize the semi-expendable account for inventory totaling ₱154,551.00;

c. Cause the issuance of ICS for all semi-expendable items distributed to end
users, as stipulated in COA Circular No. 2024-006;

d. Require the GSO to maintain an updated RegSPI, in accordance with
Section 4.7.4 of COA Circular No. 2022-004 and to submit weekly reports
of semi-expendable property issued to the accounting unit, serving as the
basis for preparing JEV; and

e. Submit promptly the required RPCI and RPCSP to the Office of the
Auditor for verification.

We recommended that Management:

a. Require all the Collecting Officers to remit their collections intact and in
full amount, daily, weekly or as soon as the amount of collection reaches
₱5,000.00 and enforce the requirement that all deposits be supported by the
RCD before remittance to the Municipal Treasurer to prevent
discrepancies, including over-deposits; and

b. Direct the Municipal Treasurer and all AOs to prepare and immediately
submit their updated cashbooks to the Office of the Auditor. Henceforth,
prepare, maintain and regularly update their respective cashbooks, as well
as, regularly reconcile the same with the accounting records so that any
items for adjustment may be corrected and resolved promptly and the
amount of their respective accountabilities is accurate and ascertainable at
any given time.

We reiterated our recommendations that Management:

a. Continue its commitment to faithfully remit all trust collections in a timely
and accurate manner to avoid penalties and ensure compliance with existing
laws, rules and regulations;

b. Prioritize the settlement of overdue obligations to prevent further
accumulation and avoid potential penalties; and

c. Investigate and adjust the excess remittance in coordination with the
concerned Agencies.

We recommended that Management:

a. Stop the practice of charging expenses that are not included in the Approved
Annual LDRRMFIP;

b. Ensure the timely and full implementation of all programmed DRRMF
PPAs and make sure that disbursement remain within the appropriated
amounts per activity as outlined in the approved LDRRMFIP;

c. Comply with the timelines set forth in the aforementioned COA Circular
for submitting the monthly Report of Sources and Utilization of
LDRRMF. Likewise, promptly submit the Year-End MDRRMF Report of
Sources and Utilization for CY 2024 to facilitate the timely verification of the
accuracy and propriety of the implemented PPAs;

d. Immediately reconcile the Trust Liabilities balance with the pertinent
accounting records and make the necessary adjustments to reflect the
correct balance in the Financial Statements (FS);

e. Immediately submit the lacking supporting documents as indicated in the
attached Annex C. Likewise, require the Accounting Office to refrain from
processing DVs without complete documentation; and

f. Require the MDRRMO to refrain from charging against the LDRRMF
those expenditures not pertaining to disaster risk reduction and
management or not in accordance with the primary purpose for which the
fund was established, otherwise, these transactions will be disallowed in
audit.

We recommended that Management direct its GAD Focal Person to:

a. Prepare a GPB that can be implemented within the year;

b. Implement properly all planned GAD activities to ensure that all GAD
gender issues raised during the year are properly addressed; and

c. Ensure that at least 5 percent of the Agencys total budget is allocated to
support the LGUs GPB.

We recommended that Management:

a. Immediately submit the lacking supporting documents as indicated in the
attached Annex E. Likewise, require the Accounting Office to refrain from
processing DVs without complete documentation; and

b. Prioritize the use of Competitive Bidding for procurement, especially for
items that are identical in nature and intended for the same purpose, to
promote transparency, efficiency and cost-effectiveness. Also, Alternative
methods of Procurement, such as SVP, must only be used when justified
under the conditions set by RA No. 9184 and its revised IRR.

Further, the Bids and Awards Committee were advised to conduct regular
reviews and monitoring of procurement activities to ensure compliance with
the procurement laws. Additionally, the LGU needs to strengthen its Annual
Procurement Plan to ensure procurement is well-planned and executed with
proper procedures.

We recommended that Management require the concerned Officials to:

a. Expedite the implementation of the PPAs funded under 20 percent DF to
maximize their intended benefits. Regular monitoring and evaluation must
be conducted to identify bottlenecks and resolve issues causing delay;

b. Submit the subject CY 2024 contracts, lacking DVs with their supporting
documents and explain for the noted deficiencies in the submitted
documents;

c. Exercise strictest scrutiny in the preparation of bidding documents and
conduct of procurement activities, especially the bid evaluation, in
accordance with the Revised IRR of RA No. 9184 and other relevant
procurement guidelines; and

d. Ensure that the contracts and payments are duly supported with
complete documentary requirements pursuant to COA Circular No.
2009-001 and COA Circular No. 2012-001.

We recommended that Management:

a. Require the LSB to ensure that prescribed planning and budgeting tools are
prepared, submitted and effectively utilized and the timelines in the
planning and budgeting process are strictly observed as prescribed under
DepEd-DBM-DILG JC No. 1, s. 2017;

b. Direct the Municipal Accountant to ensure that the supporting documents
are proper and complete before processing the claim or payment of the
same;

c. Ensure that all travel expenses are properly supported with the required
and appropriate documentation. Likewise, remind Accountable Officers to
strictly comply with COA Circular No. 2021-001, which limits the use of
certification for expenses not requiring receipts to transactions amounting
to ₱300 or less from establishments that do not issue ORs;

d. Ensure that all procurements chargeable to the SEF are processed through
direct payments to suppliers whenever possible, rather than through
reimbursement and comply with the existing tax rules and regulations; and

e. The BAC and procurement officers must ensure that the actual
procurement method used should align with what is stated in the BAC
resolution to maintain consistency and transparency. Additionally,
strengthen internal controls by requiring a clear review and approval
process before finalizing the mode of procurement in the PO. Further,
strictly abide with the guidelines on the use of appropriate alternative mode
of procurement.

We recommended that Management:

a. Strictly adhere to the prescribed guidelines on the release of retention
monies, ensuring that such funds are released only after the lapse of the one-
year defect liability period or upon submission of any acceptable surety
bond in lieu of retention, as provided under the aforementioned 2016
Revised IRR of RA No. 9184. Likewise, require the concerned contractor to
submit the necessary surety bond for retention monies already released.
Moreover, immediately submit the required Certification from the end-user
that the project is completed and inspected; and

b. Strictly comply with the posting and reporting requirements under Section
5.0 of LBC No. 154 dated February 6, 2024 to ensure transparency,
accountability and timely assessment/monitoring in the Release and
Utilization of LGSF-SBDP of the NTF-ELCAC projects.

We reiterated our previous years audit recommendation that Management
require the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer to immediately submit
the lacking DVs with complete supporting documents as well as the unsubmitted
disbursement and collection accounts for CY 2024.

Likewise, the Municipal Accountant or the accounting personnel must
undertake a thorough review of the completeness of supporting documents attached to
the DV, duly signed by authorized officials. Henceforth, adhere strictly with the
aforementioned guidelines relative to the rendition of the Agencys accounts.

We recommended that Management:

a. Review and evaluate the existing manpower resources to ensure adequate
staffing pattern that suit the present needs of the offices and create clear-
cut policies and guidelines to address the issue on the necessity and propriety
of hiring JOs/holders of COS;

b. Maximize the use of existing work force and resort to job order hiring only
in the exigency of the service and if it is not feasible for the LGU to hire said
services under a casual or contractual appointment;

c. Clearly define in the contract the nature of works or activities to be done by
the job orders/holders of COS; and

d. Require the submission of individual and quantified accomplishment
reports and definite proof of identification to support the payments and
require the Disbursing Officer to personally pay at the premises of the
Office of the Municipal Treasurer the daily/monthly wage of the job
orders/holders of COs especially those assigned in the field or in the
barangays for internal control purposes.

We recommended that Management require the Municipal Accountant and
other concerned personnel to ensure timely submission of the subsequent Financial
Statements and Reports, the paid DVs, JEVs and their supporting documents in
compliance with existing rules and regulations.

We recommended that Management:

a. Submit the approved SOs, RWOs and VOs, if any, pertaining to the delay
and extension of contract time; and

b. Strictly monitor the overall project implementation and coordinate with the
Contractor to fast track the completion of the project to ensure timely
delivery of service to its intended beneficiaries.

We recommended that Management:

a. Direct the MPMIU Engineer to prepare the monthly physical progress
reports in the implementation of the PRDP subproject to ensure that the
financial and physical progress are linked and consistently presented for the
fair presentation of the account balances and reliability of the financial
statements;
a. Require the Municipal Accountant to adjust accordingly the accounts
affected by the recognition of the value of additional work accomplished as
of year-end.

14. We recommended that the Municipal Accountant reclassify the completed projects under the CIP to the appropriate PPE accounts and accordingly recognize the corresponding prior years and current year depreciation expenses.

nature of each account and come up with the correct balances of the Cash
in Bank accounts for all funds.

We reiterated our prior years’ recommendation that Management:
a. Request the concerned officials and employees to liquidate/settle
immediately their outstanding cash advances by demanding the submission
of duly accomplished liquidation reports. Otherwise, withhold any money
due them;
b. Prepare a Journal Entry Voucher (JEV) for the liquidation of the cash
advances for Intelligence/Confidential Fund which already have
transmittal letter duly received by COA Central Office thru COA –
Intelligence and Confidential Audit Unit (ICFAU); and
c. Strengthen the operation of preventive and monitoring controls to ensure
timely submission of liquidation documents and full accounting of balances
and avoid undue accumulation of outstanding cash advances.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant exert extra efforts to
conduct verification, analysis and validation of these receivable balances, to
determine their collectability and pursue collection, refund or liquidation thereof, if
warranted. We also recommended that the Local Chief Executive file the request for
authority to write-off dormant receivable accounts which could no longer be collected
to the Commission on Audit, duly supported with the required documents.

We reiterated our previous years’ recommendations that:
a. The Inventory Team conduct complete physical inventory-taking of all
PPE and submit a complete RPCPPE not later than January 31 of each
year;
b. The Municipal General Services Officer (MGSO) and Municipal
Accountant prioritize reconciliation of the results of the inventory count
with the property and accounting records, look into the noted
discrepancies and effect/prepare the necessary adjusting entries, as the
case may be;
c. The MGSO must conduct physical inventory and inspection of all
unserviceable and obsolete items, prepare and submit the IIRUP and
facilitate the immediate disposal of those items so that they may be
dropped from the books of accounts; and
d. The Municipal Accountant must make the necessary adjustments in the
books to correct all erroneous recording/classification of PPE accounts.

We recommended that:
a. The Municipal Treasury Office and Budget Office, as the lead offices, and
the Local Finance Committee study thoroughly the reasonableness of the
projected income to be collected to arrive at a more realistic
appropriation;
b. The Municipal Treasurer must conduct periodic review to check the
collection performance level and devise remedial measures for revenue
accounts that missed the projections and effective procedures that will
improve more the collections of taxes that registered good results; and
c. The Municipal Accountant must observe proper classification of accounts.

We recommended that:
a. The LGU, through the concerned offices/departments, observe the prudent
fund management in its effort to curtail/eliminate/mitigate the spread of
COVID-19 in the locality. Henceforth, all PPAs related to COVID-19 must
be judiciously planned according to priorities considering the scarce
resources of the LGU and the urgent needs for health, safety, and
livelihood of the constituents;
b. Observe and adhere to the required posting of APP, NOA, and PPAs
implemented by the LGU to the GPPB portal for transparency and
reportorial compliance;
c. Satisfactory explanation is requested on the observations made in the
utilization and disbursement of BGCM Fund, otherwise transactions
covered by said observations will be suspended or disallowed in audit as
the case maybe; and
d. The MGSO, Municipal Disaster Risk and Reduction Management Officer
(MDRRMO) and other concerned accountable for all procurement and
subsequent distribution of food reliefs, are requested to fully account and
submit inventory report on the food reliefs received, distributed and on
hand.

We recommended that the LSB:
1. Obtain/secure the required DepEd-approved SIP, DEDP and other
information from the DepEd RO on the allocation of each school from the
National Budget as contained in the NEP, which supposedly were the bases
for the preparation and approval by the LSB of the SEF Budget, copy
furnishing the Audit Team, for reference. Henceforth, the LSB must
strictly observe the requirements for the preparation for the SEF on the
basis of the revised guidelines on the use of SEF.
2. The obligated amount of ₱0.53 million without legal and valid supporting
documents shall be reverted back to unutilized fund and subject to new
budgeting/appropriation for SEF; and
3. Institute strong control measures in all procurements. All supporting
documents must be reviewed as to details and completeness of information
in order for them to acquire the effect of legality, validity and sufficiency of
documents supporting a public procurement.

We recommended that the LGU observe strictly the laws and regulations on
the judicious planning and utilization of LDRRMF by utilizing the fund only for
those allowed and legitimate PPAs to avoid depleting the fund for the expenditures
outside the purpose for its creation. Explanation is also requested as to why Section
4(u) of RA No. 11494, DBM LBC No. 124, and Item 3 of GPPB Resolution No. 18-
2020 were not observed for the budgetary realignment of fund, procurements and
disbursements and the required posting to GPPB portal and other places.

We recommended that Management:
a. Prepare the 1) Summary/List of Donations Received, Distributed and
Balances for In-kind Donations; 2)Report on the Receipt and Utilization of
Cash Donations; 3)Report on the Receipt and Distribution/Issuance of
Donated Relief Goods; 4) Report on the Receipt and Distribution/Issuance
of PPE and copy furnish NDRRMC therewith, through the OCD, also copy
furnishing the auditor within 10 working days following the end of each
quarter, pursuant to COA Circular Nos. 2014-002 and 2020-009; and
b. Record donation in the SL in accordance with Section IV.B.7 of COA
Circular 2014-002 dated April 15, 2014 and to reclassify the account from
Trust-Other Payables account to Trust-Liabilities- DRRMF.

We recommended to the Local Chief Executive to:
a. Strictly enforce and intensify the information campaign on “no
segregation, no collection” policy among residents and consider imposing
fines and penalties for violators thereof;
b. Ensure that the composition of the SWMB complies with Section 12 of RA
No. 9003;
c. Encourage and provide needed assistance to the remaining 17 barangays to
establish their own MRFs for the collection, segregation, recycling of
biodegradable, recyclable, compostable and reusable wastes; and
d. Allocate funds for the construction and operation of a sanitary landfill as a
final disposal site for solid wastes.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the GAD Focal Point
System (GFPS) or its equivalent officers/employees to:
a. Strictly comply with the existing rules and regulations prescribing the
guidelines in the formulation of Annual GPB, development of gender
issues, GAD activities, and implementation and monitoring of the GPB.
Mainstreaming of regular PPAs must be made through the use of HGDG
assessment tools to justify the attribution of the portion or whole costs to
GAD budget;
b. Avoid the general statements of the gender issue which do not necessarily
reflect gender differences in the situation of women and men. The gender
issue must be specific and supported by evidence such as sex disaggregated
data and gender analysis results;
c. Utilize the GAD Fund only for acceptable PPAs that would address the
identified gender issues in the locality based on the RA No. 9710 or the
Magna Carta of Women, PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA JMC No. 2013-01, as
amended, and other relevant international commitments; and
d. Stop the granting of additional benefits to LGU employees without legal
basis at the disguise of GAD activity.

We recommended the following:
a. Implement immediately and on timely basis the identified PPAs under the
20 per cent DF. The Municipal Planning and Development Officer and
Municipal Engineer must monitor the implementation of the projects to
fast tract its completion;
b. The members of the Bids and Awards Committee schedule early all
procurements activities pertaining to development projects to pave way for
their timely implementation and completion;
c. All 20 per cent DF projects must be effectively planned and the same must
be procurement-and implementation-ready; and
d. Observe the proper realignment of appropriated budget pursuant to law to
avoid legal issue that may hamper the socio-economic of the Municipality.

Also, require the AOs concerned to renew their fidelity bonds before the expiration and ensure that their bonds be sufficient to cover their actual accountabilities as required under Treasury Circular No. 02-009. Those whose maximum cash accountability have already increased must file their application for increase in the amount of bond.

We recommended that Management:

Require the Officer-In-Charge/Supply Officer to submit to the Auditor, on a monthly basis, the Monthly Reports of Fuel Consumption, Summary of Requisition and Issue Vouchers and Monthly Report of Official Travels for verification and audit purposes.

We recommended that Management:

Justify why the Municipality resorted to Shopping instead of public bidding having a total cost of ₱101,200 which exceeded its threshold if procurement of same items were consolidated; and
Direct the Municipal Official/Employees concerned to explain the deficiencies noted on the particular disbursements.

We recommended that Management:

Instruct the Inventory Committee to conduct physical count of all existing PPEs and provide their complete description. Also, prepare the RPCPPE by type and proper format; and
Require both the Accounting Office and the Inventory Committee to reconcile the accounting records and the property records and effect the necessary adjustments to present the correct financial position of the agency.

We reiterated our recommendation that Management prepare and submit the IIRUP and immediately dispose the said properties to prevent further deterioration and decline in their value and to generate additional income from their sale, if any.

We recommended that:
The Local Finance Committee and the Budget Office as the lead office:
Conduct a systematic and realistic forecasting of revenue taking into account prior years’ experience and statistics as aide in setting targets;
Review the present budgeting to set a clearer guide for the revenue generating Departments towards attaining income projections; and
Provide for the targets on income accounts that did not have projections which must be based on statistics, prior years’ experience and future expectation.
Periodic review must be made by concerned municipal officials to ascertain whether collection targets are attained.  The causes for the shortfall in collections must be analyzed and alternative measures must be looked into to avoid incurrence of misses in projections in the future.

We recommended that:
The existing articles and provisions of the Revenue Code of the Municipality of Dimasalang be revisited with the end-in-view of considering the call of the present times towards the best interest of the public and the government; and
The existing tax rates, fees and charges prescribed in the Revenue Code be updated to increase the revenue collection of local taxes and be able to sustain an adequate, appropriate and relevant tax rates in relation to the Municipality’s fast-paced growth and development requirements.

We recommended that the Treasury Office and all its collectors strictly implement the pertinent provisions of Article 3 of the Local Revenue Code by requiring CTC taxpayers to file a sworn declaration of total earnings derived from business, compensation, exercise of profession and income from real property during the preceding year.  Subsequently, this sworn declaration must be the basis for assessing and collecting the correct amount of community tax.

Accomplishment Reports of PPAs programmed to be implemented for FY 2018 based on the approved LGU budget, in compliance with Item 4.1.2 of the same DBM Budget Circular, in order to substantiate the grant of CNA Incentive to its officers and employees.

We recommended that management strictly comply and immediately submit the documentary requirements to support the payment of the purchased lot as prescribed under PD No. 1445 and COA Circular No. 2012-001.

We recommended that Management require the Bids and Awards Committee, through its Secretariat to submit the necessary documentary requirements to support honoraria claims. Also, the Municipal Accountant ensure the completeness of supporting documents before approving any disbursement voucher for payment.

We recommended that:

The concerned officials and employees observe the timelines in the preparation and approval of the SEF budget for the ensuing year.

We recommended that the members of the Local School Board include allocation for the ECCD Program on the succeeding SEF Budget and to utilize the same according to the authorized purposes as required under Section 7 of RA No. 10410 and DepEd – DBM – DILG Joint Circular No. 1, s. 2017.
